characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: The main protagonist of the series. Henry is a selfish, greedy troublemaker who likes to play tricks on people. However, several hints in the TV series imply that he isn't quite as horrid as everyone makes him out to be. Abusive Offspring: In the books, he is abusive to his parents whenever they don't let him have something that he wants.Henry:You're the meanest, most horrible parents in the world and I hate you! Adaptational Karma: There are certain stories in the books where he's aKarma Houdini, but in the TV series, some of these stories actually change it to him getting punished."Horrid Henry's Dance Class": In the book, he's kicked out of Miss Tutu's class for spoiling the performance which to him wasn't really apunishmentbecause it meant he could go to Karate Class but in the TV show, he ends up having to go to dance class twice a week."Horrid Henry's Hobby": Henry received little, if any, punishment for trashing the cereal aisle all for some golden gizmos at the end of the story, but at the end of the cartoon version, it turns out that he can't trade any of them with his classmates because they have golden gizmos themselves. "Horrid Henry's Dance Class": In the book, he's kicked out of Miss Tutu's class for spoiling the performance which to him wasn't really apunishmentbecause it meant he could go to Karate Class but in the TV show, he ends up having to go to dance class twice a week. "Horrid Henry's Hobby": Henry received little, if any, punishment for trashing the cereal aisle all for some golden gizmos at the end of the story, but at the end of the cartoon version, it turns out that he can't trade any of them with his classmates because they have golden gizmos themselves.

characters.name: Horrid Henry
characters.description: The main protagonist of the series. Henry is a selfish, greedy troublemaker who likes to play tricks on people. However, several hints in the TV series imply that he isn't quite as horrid as everyone makes him out to be. Abusive Offspring: In the books, he is abusive to his parents whenever they don't let him have something that he wants.Henry:You're the meanest, most horrible parents in the world and I hate you! Adaptational Karma: There are certain stories in the books where he's aKarma Houdini, but in the TV series, some of these stories actually change it to him getting punished."Horrid Henry's Dance Class": In the book, he's kicked out of Miss Tutu's class for spoiling the performance which to him wasn't really apunishmentbecause it meant he could go to Karate Class but in the TV show, he ends up having to go to dance class twice a week."Horrid Henry's Hobby": Henry received little, if any, punishment for trashing the cereal aisle all for some golden gizmos at the end of the story, but at the end of the cartoon version, it turns out that he can't trade any of them with his classmates because they have golden gizmos themselves. "Horrid Henry's Dance Class": In the book, he's kicked out of Miss Tutu's class for spoiling the performance which to him wasn't really apunishmentbecause it meant he could go to Karate Class but in the TV show, he ends up having to go to dance class twice a week. "Horrid Henry's Hobby": Henry received little, if any, punishment for trashing the cereal aisle all for some golden gizmos at the end of the story, but at the end of the cartoon version, it turns out that he can't trade any of them with his classmates because they have golden gizmos themselves.

characters.name: Perfect Peter
characters.description: Henry's younger brother and main foil. In stark contrast to Henry, he is organised, polite and well-behaved, bordering on obsequious. However, in spite of his name, he can be a bit 'horrid' himself — he is something of a tattletale who takes pleasure in seeing Henry being punished. Adaptational Nice Guy: In the live-action movie, hisBitch in Sheep's Clothingtendencies are removed (or at the very least, toned down), and he's more genuinely nice. Annoying Younger Sibling: Henry will stop at nothing to prevent Peter from bothering him. Anti-Role Model: Peter is not as perfect as you may think. Attention Whore: Having been rewarded so much for his good behaviour his whole life, Peter has been conditioned to expect praise and attention for even the most minor things such as using a fork or “being normal.”His parents paying more attention to Henry than him during one mealtime is what drives him to behave more like his older brother for the day in "Perfect Peter's Horrid Day".

characters.name: Mum
characters.description: Abusive Parents: Downplayed, but she and Dad always blame Henry, even when he does nothing at all.In "Happy Birthday, Peter" she seemingly cheers at the idea of Henry disappearing forever...in front of everyone.Hilarious how it is done though. However; the mom does quickly look on with guilt when everyone else looks at her. Henry, as usual, does not even care. In "Happy Birthday, Peter" she seemingly cheers at the idea of Henry disappearing forever...in front of everyone.Hilarious how it is done though. However; the mom does quickly look on with guilt when everyone else looks at her. Henry, as usual, does not even care. Hilarious how it is done though. However; the mom does quickly look on with guilt when everyone else looks at her. Henry, as usual, does not even care. Adaptational Attractiveness: Even considering the fact that Henry is decidedlynot the most reliable POV person, she is never made out to be very good-looking in the books, and the illustrations make her look average at best. In the TV series, she's rather pretty. Adaptational Jerkass: In the books, she at least had reasons to be hard on Henry because he was significantly harder to deal with there. In the animated series, because most of Henry’s cruelties had been excised, the Mum comes across as being harder on Henry even though he hadn’t done anything wrong.

characters.name: Silly Simon (Dad)
characters.description: Abusive Parents: Also downplayed. He and Mum are always blaming Henry when he didn't even do anything at all, or what happened was completely out of his control. Adaptational Attractiveness: Even ignoring Henry's unfavourable descriptions of him, the illustrations in Francesca Simon's books portray Simon as rather ugly with thin, balding hair and an enormous carrot nose. In the TV series, he's pretty normal-looking. Butt-Monkey: He's the second most likely in the household to fall victim to Henry's pranks. Catchphrase:"Don't behorrid, Henry" "Don't behorrid, Henry"

characters.name: Moody Margaret Millicent Maudita Smythe
characters.description: Margaret Millicent Maudita Smythe is the next door neighbour of Henry's family, as well as Henry's classmate and arch nemesis. She is very vain and is spoiled by her parents, who she treats like slaves. Abusive Offspring: Margaret is vile to her parents. She treats them like slaves, verbally abuses them and belittles them to get her way. They have only been shown to discipline their daughter once, and that was when Margaret had been naughty to Henry’s parents and they thought she was so bad that they sent an Email to complain about her (in reality it was Henry) and after that they never punish her again. The bad thing is that Margaret’s parents are both kind, easy-going, lenient, friendly and insecure, and they spoil their daughter anyway, yet Margaret still bullies them. Adaptation Dye-Job: Margaret's hair colour changes depending on the medium. In the books, Tony Ross's full colour illustrations depict her as having brown hair like Henry. In the television series, however, her hair is redish-pink, and in the live-action Horrid Henry movie, she is ginger. Alpha Bitch: Margaret is very popular with the girls in her class, being a star football player at Ashton Primary, having access to pretty much anything with her wealth, and having lots in common with most of the girls in her class. She's also callous, cruel, and treats Henry especially poorly. Big Bad: She's Henry's main rival and is also the biggest jerk in the series.

characters.name: Rude Ralph
characters.description: Adaptational Nice Guy: He's nicer and less mean-spirited in the show than in the book series. Adaptational Relationship Overhaul: SomewhatDownplayedin Season 1, less downplayed and played straighter in Series 2 and beyond. In the book series, while they tolerate each other, it's more due to proximity and their friendship is more shallow, as they are willing make fun of one another, and the relationship is more about what can they bring to the chaotic table. Series 1 wasTruer to the Textin this regard, as both Henry and Ralph are frenemies, ditching each other when trouble rises for them. Series 2 and beyond has him be Henry’s best friend, being more consistently loyal to one another, and having a healthier relationship. Ambiguously Gay:At least in Series 2 and later in the TV show, Ralph and Henry are much closer than guy best friends are normally, enjoy being each other’s dance partners, sometimes hold hands in public, act like cute dorks when they’re together, and are overall very attached to each other. However, he was a bit uncomfortable when Henry took a picture of the two of them kissing. Maybe he didn’t want people to see them, or wasn’t ready for them to go public? Birds of a Feather: With Henry. They share the same interests, are blunt and rude boys, and are hinted to have genuine feelings for each other. Characterization Marches On: In Series 1, Ralph appeared to be a close friend of Henry's, but he was still quick to desert him several times, laughing at him along with the rest of the class whenever Henry was humiliatednoteThis iscloser in regards to the original book, as both Henry and Ralph mostly tolerate each other. In Series 2 and later seasons, their friendship seems to be stronger; they are now best friends. They prove to have a strong bond and stand by each other. Ralph also seems to be defending Henry multiple times.

characters.name: Sour Susan
characters.description: Adaptation Dye-Job: In the original books, her hair is blonde, as seen in full-colour illustrations by Tony Ross. In the television series, however, she has ginger hair. Adaptational Ugliness: Susan is made to be more homely (having curly ginger hair, pale skin, and a puckered-up face) in the cartoon just to match her sour personality. The books had her look normal, with long blonde hair and good taste in fashion, arguably being much prettier than Margaret. Butt-Monkey: Margaret treats her more like a slave than a friend, and verbally abuses her as much as she does to Henry. The Ditz: She comes off as this when she's repeating everything that Margaret says, much to the latter's annoyance. The Dragon: She primarily acts as Margaret’s reluctant sidekick.

characters.name: Aerobic Al
characters.description: Best Friend: Henry's best friend after Ralph in the TV series. Catchphrase: "Cool!" or "Not cool.", which he says a lot to the point where they border onVerbal Tics(he pronounces "cool" as "kewl"). Fire-Forged Friends: Al appears to be friends with Gorgeous Gurinder, despite them having little in common; Al being an athletic guy who likes gross things likeGross Class Zero, while Gurinder is a vain and conventionally feminine friend of Margaret’s. Huge Schoolgirl:Gender inverted. He's the tallest of Henry's classmates. Lovable Jock: Al is very athletic, enjoys playing sports (especially football), and is one of Henry’s closest, most loyal friends.

characters.name: Beefy Bert
characters.description: Catchphrase: "I dunno..." The Ditz: He doesn't seem to be very bright, since he literally just says one thing for when he doesn't get something, which isanything. Dumb Is Good: Bert isn't very intelligent, but his heart's in the right place, and despite being "beefy", he'sfarfrom a brute. Fat Idiot: In additon to being airheaded, Bert's rather chubby. Gentle Giant: Despite his intimidating appearance, he's not violent.

characters.name: Brainy Brian
characters.description: Black and Nerdy: They don’t call him “Brainy” for nothing. He is also socially awkward and he lisps. Gentleman and a Scholar: The smartest kid in Henry's class and one of the politest. Mad Scientist: In his more comical moments he can tend to be this. He was even laughing maniacally while describing one of his science experiments in one episode. Platonic Life-Partners: With Clever Clare in the books. Token Minority: The only non-white member in the Purple Hand Gang.

characters.name: Greedy Graham
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn’t appear in the live-action movie. Extreme Omnivore: Apparently ate aGross Class Zero comic book. In the words of Al, "don't ask". Fat Bastard: Inverted. He's fat and greedy, but not heartless. Odd Friendship: With Aerobic Al. He's fat and unathletic, and Al is the complete opposite. Trademark Favorite Food: He particularly loves carrot sticks, since he is most commonly seen eating them when he appears. He evensays that carrots are his "trademark"in one episode.

characters.name: Anxious Andrew
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn’t appear in the live-action movie. Ambiguously Gay: Much like Henry and Ralph, Andrew and William are much closer than guy best friends are normally, they have similar personalities, are rarely seen apart, get upset when they are, and have a similar fashion sense. Break the Cutie: That ultimately seems to be Henry’s goal when dealing with him. Butt-Monkey: Henry tends to antagonize him the most of all the kids in the class. Despite this, Andrew is friends with Henry, and they get along better after Series 1. The Cutie: Andrew is as sweet as he is insecure.

characters.name: Weepy William
characters.description: Ambiguously Gay: Much like Henry and Ralph, Andrew and William are much closer than guy best friends are normally, they have similar personalities, are rarely seen apart, get upset when they are, and have a similar fashion sense. Break the Cutie: Poor William can’t seem to catch a break from crying. Butt-Monkey: Along with Andrew, Henry tends to antagonize him the most out of all the kids in his class. However, after Series 1, he and Henry become close friends. The Cutie: A sweet, friendly, emotional kid without a bad bone in his body. Hide Your Lesbians: Given how close William and Andrew are, it seems there is more to their relationship than meets the eye.

characters.name: Lazy Linda
characters.description: Asleep in Class: Part of the reason for her nickname is that she's rarely seen awake in class. Brainy Brunette: Linda has brown hair and isimplied to be very intelligent. Miss Battle-Axe once cornered her with a difficult maths question, and though she had been asleep, Linda managed to answer perfectly when waking up (possibly not even hearing Miss Battle-Axe). Lazy Bum: She's not called "Lazy Linda" for nothing. She is almost always seen sleeping and rarely puts in the effort to do anything. Like Is, Like, a Comma: Slips in 'like' in, like, almost every sentence. Messy Hair: Her hair is a wreck, due to never taking the time to fix it.

characters.name: Gorgeous Gurinder and Singing Soraya
characters.description: All Girls Want Bad Boys: They seem to have crushes on Ralph and Henry. Ambiguously Bi: Although Gurinder and Soraya do show friendships with the boys and maybe even crushes on Henry and Ralph, they are obviously very close with each other. They are comfortable sleeping in the same bed, as shown at Moody Margaret's sleepover. Aroused by Their Voice: Singing Soraya certainly has a beautiful, soft, and gentle singing voice. Brainless Beauty: Sometimes, but Gurinder is most likely. Brainy Brunette: Soraya is revealed to be good at maths and musically gifted, and has black hair.

characters.name: Jolly Josh
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn’t appear in the live-action movie. Class Clown: His role in the series is to prank people, though he's more harmless than Henry is and is more of a lighthearted prankster than an outright bully. Good Counterpart: He's basically Henry if his horridness was removed and his pranking was harmless. Living Prop: Despite being one of Henry's friends, he is usually in the background and rarely, if ever, has a major role in an episode. He is also notably the only boy in Henry's class not to be a member of the Purple Hand Gang. Nice Guy: Pranking aside, he's relatively friendly and lives up to his nickname by being, well, jolly.

characters.name: The Best Boys Club
characters.description: A Day in the Limelight: "Horrid Henry and the Detention Club" is this for Tidy Ted. Butt-Monkey: Out of the boys, Ted seems to be the one who is most commonly antagonized by other characters, especially Henry and Margaret. Even the Loving Hero Has Hated Ones: The three of them are genuinely nice kids for the most part, but Tidy Ted is shown to dislike Moody Margaret because of her cruel behavior. Hidden Depths: It is implied that they actually don’t like Peter because of how badly he treats them. Jerkass Ball: Goody Gordon grabs it in the book version of "Perfect Peter's Revenge" wherein he takes part in teasing Peter about how Henry had tricked him into thinking that he'd gone into the future.

characters.name: Nasty Nicola
characters.description: Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: While it's obvious from her name that she's not a good person at all, it is initially believed that she is aNice Girl, until shestarts framing Henry for crimes she committed. Canon Foreigner: She never appeared in the books. Evil Is Petty: She took advantage of Henry and framed him for absolutely no reason at all! Frame-Up: Framed Henry for crimes she committed. Hate Sink: Just look at all her tropes! She’s worse than all of Henry’s enemies combined.

characters.name: Clever Claire
characters.description: Age Lift: While she was around Henry's age in the books, in the series, she's younger than Henry. Only Sane Woman: In the book version of "Horrid Henry's Injection," she was the only one of the kids who called Henry out for acting hysterical about the injection while the other kids were throwing fits about it.

characters.name: Miss Boudicca Battle-Axe
characters.description: Henry's teacher, whom he dislikes due to being overly strict. She appears to be Scottish, due to speaking with a Scottish accent and playing the bagpipes. Butt-Monkey: Henry loves to play tricks on her, and her students don't respect her enough. She also has to deal with an overbearing mother. Freudian Excuse: With students as reckless as hers and an abusive mother, you would be miserable too. Four Eyes, Zero Soul: While not malicious, she is aStern Teacherwho wears glasses. Jerkass Ball: In some episodes, she borders onSadist Teacher, such as when she gave Henry a double detentionjust for saying that he doesn't like "Boodle Poodles", a show that she likes, and rigging the school election so that Henry doesn't win by hiding his votes.

characters.name: Mrs. Cynthia Oddbod
characters.description: Nice Girl: Although Mrs. Oddbod is quite a strict principal, she is fair and does not generally dislike the students like Miss Battle-Axe and Soggy Sid do. She only gets mad at the students when they're causing trouble. Only Sane Woman: She’s always ready to see Henry and Margaret in her office when they cause trouble. Reasonable Authority Figure: She is far more amicable and friendly towards Henry compared to Ms. Battle-Axe and Soggy Sid. Token Good Teammate: The kindest adult on the show, along with Walter and Miss Lovely.

characters.name: Soggy Sidmouth Soggington
characters.description: Ascended Extra: He only appears in one story in the books, but is a major recurring character in the series. Catchphrase: “You horrid boy!” Even Evil Has Loved Ones: "Evil" might be stretching it, but he is very fond of Miss Battle-Axe as well as his pet goldfish, Goldilocks. Improperly Paranoid: He likely hates Henry more in Series 4-5 because he believes that he's up to no good, even though Henry is less horrid than usual. Insufferable Imbecile: On top of being extremely strict and aggressive, he is also shown to be quite dim-witted and unreasonable, which can be best exemplified when he berates his students for running when they were actuallywalking, and when he insists that they aren't dressed appropriately to go outside in the snow when they clearlyare.

characters.name: Miss Lovely
characters.description: Adaptation Dye-Job: In the books she has blond hair, in the TV show, she has black hair. All-Loving Hero: As her name states, Miss Lovely is very lovely and sees the good in everyone, even Henry. Beware the Nice Ones: Although Miss Lovely is, well, lovely, she won't tolerate bullying behavior under her watch, as shown in "Horrid Henry and the Lucky Thing" when she is actually seen angry and sternly addresses Margaret by herfull nameafter she threw things at Henry during her lesson. Cool Teacher: In contrast to Miss Battle-Axe, she’s more friendly and helpful. Hair of Gold, Heart of Gold: In the books, she has blonde hair.

characters.name: Wheely Walter
characters.description: Cool Teacher: The only teacher whom Henry actually likes and doesn’t treat badly. It helps that he’s actually competent at his job, unlike most of the other teachers. Don't You Dare Pity Me!: Subverted. Walter doesn’t like being pitied and actually likes Henry’s nickname for him, but he was glad when people stood up to a director for discriminating against him. Inspirationally Disadvantaged: Usually averted, but in the episode where he’sdiscriminated from a movie because of his disability, everyone (includingHenry) quits because of it. Intergenerational Friendship: With Henry. We are not joking. Nice Guy: He's a kind science teacher to Henry and his students.

characters.name: Madame Tutu
characters.description: Accent Slip-Up: While she usually speaks with a French accent, it is actually fake and she drops it a few times, particularly when she is talking to Henry. Adaptational Name Change: In the books, she was known as Miss Impatience Tutu, while in the show, she is referred to as Madame Tutu, with her first name never being said. Adaptational Nice Girl: Tutu was established as aPerpetual FrownerChild Haterin the original books who didn't like Henry. In the series, she genuinely cares about her students, even Henry, and is more jovial. French Jerk: Subverted, since a.), she's not actually French, and b.), while she is aStern Teacher, she is just doing her job and genuinely wants everyone in her class to succeed.

characters.name: Greasy Greta Minus, the Demon Dinner Lady
characters.description: Adaptation Expansion: In the book, Henry defeats her by feeding her biscuits laced with hot sauce. In the cartoon, she nullifies the spice by drinking water, and it takes anEnemy Minebetween all the students to run her out. Even Evil Has Loved Ones: She won't take any sweets from her daughter, Patsy. Evil Is Petty: Taking sweets from the kids may be enforcing school rules, but eating them herself certainly isn’t. Fat Bastard: She clearly doesn’t work off the sweets she eats. Hate Sink: All the kids hate her so much that they put aside any differences just to get back at her.

characters.name: Stuck-Up Steve
characters.description: Bratty Half-Pint: Just like his equally horrid cousin, maybe even more so. Costume Evolution: Starting with Series 5, he now wears a green blazer over his purple sweater, which originally went from purple to green between his appearances. Evil Redhead: You could say he's like a male version of Moody Margaret. Laser-Guided Karma: Steve usually gets comeuppance for his mean behaviour. Lonely Rich Kid: Averted in the books where he is friends with Bossy Bill as Bill moved to Steve’s school after the events ofHorrid Henry’s Arch Enemy, and Steve is also friends with many other classmates of his, like Hoity-Toity Tim, but in the show, he’s never seen with any friends at all until series 5, and the two friends he has are quite stupid and copy everything Steve says. Makes you wonder why he and his mother invite their relatives over so often.

characters.name: Rich Aunt Ruby
characters.description: Always Someone Better: Ruby has fabulous wealth and tons of amazing stuff to show off, unlike Mum, who is a much more conventional citizen. Big Brother Bully: Well, Big Sister. Usually, when she invites Mum over for tea, it’s just an excuse to rub her wealth in her face. Innocently Insensitive: She's not afraid to gloat about her wealth to Mum, though she doesn't really mean any harm. Rich Bitch: Always feeling the need to gloat about her fabulous wealth to her sister. Took a Level in Jerkass: In the first season, she at least tried to be polite towards her relatives and had moments where she acted somewhat reasonable towards Henry. This fades away later on, becoming a lot more snooty, strict, and demanding.

characters.name: Great Aunt Greta
characters.description: Accidental Misnaming: She always mistakes Henry for a little girl named "Henrietta", no matter how many times they correct her. This isTruth in Television, since some elders tend to lose their hearing and seeing, causing them to mistake one thing for another. Characterization Marches On: The first two series of the show portray her as just being a stereotypical senile old lady whose only characterization revolves around her poor eyesight and hearing, resulting in her mistakening Henry for a little girl and Peter for a teenager. But starting from Series 3 and onwards, her character gets expanded more and showcases her to be something of aCool Old Ladywho can be surprisingly athletic and talented in a lot of games. Cool Old Lady: Even though she mistakes Henry for a girl and Peter for a teenager, she is great at yo-yo tricks, sold a rare stamp for a small fortune, and is a great rope skipper. Cloudcuckoolander: She’s pretty weird and freaky, but Henry doesn’t mind (as long as it doesn't affect him in any way). The Ghost: In the books, she was just mentioned, but we never met her until the show.

characters.name: Prissy Polly Peregrine (née Penelope)
characters.description: Brainless Beauty: She’s not very bright. Break the Cutie: She cries after Henry sabotages her wedding and laughs at her being covered in cake. Brooding Boy, Gentle Girl: A perky woman who's engaged with a grumpy man. Cute, but Cacophonic: She is rather attractive, but also has a very shrill voice that tends to getloudquickly. Distaff Counterpart: You can probably see where Peter gets his whininess from.

characters.name: Pimply Paul Peregrine
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn’t appear in the live-action movie. Awful Wedded Life: It’s pretty clear that Paul and Polly aren’t very happy together. Brooding Boy, Gentle Girl: A grumpy man who's engaged with a perky woman. Butt-Monkey: Dealing with an overbearing wife, a baby who pukes all the time, and a mischievous cousin-in-law isn't the easiest job in the world, nor is his job as a milkman. Child Hater: Henry believes him to be this, especially since he mumbled it at his and Polly’s wedding, but that was just because Henry was acting up.

characters.name: Vomiting Vera Venicia Verincular
characters.description: Baby Talk: She is always babbling happily. Big Brother Worship: She seems to love Henry more than Peter. Creepy Child: She seems to actually throw up on purpose. The Cutie: Vera's adorableness truly stands out, especially in this show. Cute Mute: She doesn’t speak, but it doesn’t make her less endearing.

characters.name: Fussy Uncle Francis
characters.description: Foolish Sibling, Responsible Sibling: Zigzagged. Despite seemingly coming off as more grounded and disciplined than his brother Silly Simon (Dad), he can be just as silly and immature as him. Jerkass to One: He only seems to hold some animosity towards Simon, as he seems to get along with the rest of the family. Manchild: Much like his brother Simon, he still acts rather childish despite being an adult. Neat Freak: Judging by him inspecting Henry's household for dust, he appears to be one. Sibling Rivalry: He and Simon still harbour a grudge towards each other.

characters.name: Henry’s Grandparents
characters.description: Adapted Out: The only major characters to not appear in the show. It’s mentioned that Grandma died when Henry was a toddler (probably about Vera's age). Only Sane Man: They’re much more grounded and sensible than the rest of Henry’s family.

characters.name: Doddery Donald
characters.description: Cool Old Guy: He is an old man who plays Air Guitar and used to be called Dancing Donald. What Happened to the Mouse?: He is Great Aunt Greta’s brother and made 2 other appearances. He was seen in a flashback with Old Olive, which could mean they are married, and in the Old People’s Home, suggesting he lives there. You’d think he might get more attention as Great Aunt Greta’s brother, and as such Mum’s maternal uncle and Henry and Peter’s great uncle.

characters.name: Bossy Bill
characters.description: Broken Ace: Before we meet him, he's said to be a perfect child. Think again. Broken Pedestal: When his Dad, Bossy Bob finds out that he had been getting Henry into trouble all day and copied his own bottom, he tells him to his face that he's "Not the son he thought he was." Chuck Cunningham Syndrome: Vanishes in Series 4 and 5. Frame-Up: He blames Henry for a lot of the bad things he did. Evil Brit: Inverted. He and his dad are the only characters that are given American accents in the audiotapes. Played straight in the TV series though, where they have heavy Scouse accents.

characters.name: Mr. Nerdon
characters.description: Adaptational Backstory Change: In the books, he was initially Henry's teacher before Henry caused him to get hospitalized leading to Miss Battle Axe taking his position. In the TV show, he's substituting for Miss Battle Axe while she is sick. Fainting: In the book version, he faints after Henry's final prank on him. (Which consists of him making Mr. Nerdon think that he dropped Peter out of the window to his death.) Stern Teacher: He is the toughest, meanest, nastiest and baddest teacher in Henry's school and is very strict. Unfortunate Names: Henry even makes fun of his name at one point.

characters.name: Rabid Rebecca
characters.description: Babysitter from Hell: As Henry puts it, she’s the worst babysitter in the world. She bullies and enslaves anyone who is a kid. The Brute: She is willing to hurt little kids, no matter what they do. Child Hater: All she does when babysitting the kids is bully them. Fat Bitch: She’s tubby and one of the villains. Hate Sink: She’s worse than any other villain in the show.

characters.name: Vic Van Wrinkle
characters.description: Amoral Afrikaner: His last name and the fact he has African items in his office imply he is of Afrikaner descent like his actor Knight of Cerebus: The mood darkens when he is around. The Von Trope Family: Has Van right in his last name.

characters.name: Fang
characters.description: Canon Foreigner: He was made for the TV series and doesn’t exist in the books. Catchphrase: “Yeah yeah yeah!” Jerk with a Heart of Gold: Fang may be temperamental, mischievous, and outright vicious at times, but he is a loyal pet to Henry and even gets on well with Fluffy occasionally. Suddenly Speaking: While his "speech" is usually limited to a raspy, gravelly "Yeah, yeah, yeah!", in "Horrid Henry and the Snotslimer Redemption", he is shown inexplicably capable of speaking in full sentences to other hamsters in adeep, smooth American accent.

characters.name: Dizzy Dave
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series despite being mentioned a lot, and is one of Henry's best friends in the books. The Klutz: As his name says, he spins around a lot and knocks things over.

characters.name: Tough Toby
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series like Dave even though he is one of Henry's best friends in the books. Manly Man: He's depicted as strong and tough.

characters.name: Fiery Fiona
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series. Hair-Trigger Temper: She gets angry and hotheaded a lot more than even Henry.

characters.name: Vain Violet
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series. Narcissist: She loves her appearance a lot.

characters.name: Kung-Fu Kate
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series. Girly Girl with a Tomboy Streak: She's a martial arts girl who's just as feminine as her female classmates.

characters.name: Kind Kasim
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series. Even the Loving Hero Has Hated Ones: He despises Henry since he's his exact opposite, even warning Henry to stop being so horrid or he'd teach him a lesson he'd never forget in "Perfect Peter's Revenge". Nice Guy: It's in his name after all.

characters.name: Stone-Age Steven
characters.description: Adapted Out: Doesn't appear in the TV series. Cannot Tell Fiction from Reality: He genuinely thinks that he's a caveman.
